coredata.dc:title: Iron-doped carbon dots with peroxidase activity for colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal detection of tannic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Xiang
Qu, Xiaowei
Rehman, Muneeb Ur
Hu, Zhiyuan
Wu, Fengshou
coredata.dc:description: Given the indispensable role and diverse applications of tannic acid in the food industry, the development of an efficient, precise, and rapid detection and measurement technology for tannic acid is of paramount importance. In this research, we presented the fabrication of iron-incorporated carbon dots (Fe-CDs) through a single-step hydrothermal method, employing Schiff base-structured iron complexes (Fe-Salen) and citric acid (CA) as precursors. Leveraging the catalytic properties of Fe-CDs, we developed a strategy that integrates colorimetric and fluorescence dual signal detection techniques to detect tannic acid (TA). At the heart of this strategy is the peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity exhibited by Fe-CDs, which effectively catalyzes the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into its visually discernible blue oxidized form (ox-TMB), in the existence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Upon the addition of TA, the blue solution undergoes discoloration, enabling colorimetric detection. Furthermore, the interaction between Fe-CDs, TMB, and H2O2 significantly quenches the luminescence of Fe-CDs, and the recovery of fluorescence upon the addition of TA introduces an additional dimension of fluorescence signal to the detection process. This dual-signal detection mode not only enhances the sensitivity and accuracy of the assay but also broadens the potential applications of Fe-CDs in fields such as biological analysis and food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tannic acid
Carbon dots
Peroxidase-like activity
Dual-signal sensing

Detection of TA in actual samples

X. Deng acknowledges the financial support from the 15th Graduate Innovative Fund of Wuhan Institute of Technology (grant no. CX2023005 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Transmission Electron Microscopy image; (B) FT-IR spectrum; (C) High-resolution scan of C 1 s; (D) N 1 s; (E) O 1 s; and (F) Fe 2p of Fe-CDs.

(A) Spectra in the UV–vis range of Fe-CDs and Fe-Salen; (B) Fluorescent spectra of Fe-CDs under excitation from 330 to 370 nm; (C) Photoluminescence spectrum of Fe-CDs; (D) Linear plots between absorbance and integrated fluorescence intensity of Quinine sulfate; and (E) Fe-CDs. (Data were presented as mean ± SD n = 3).

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of (A) Fe-CDs, Fe-CDs + H2O2, TMB + H2O2, and Fe-CDs + TMB + H2O2; (B) Fe-CDs, Fe-CDs + H2O2, o-PD + H2O2, and Fe-CDs + o-PD + H2O2. (C) The Michaelis-Menten curve of TMB and (E) H2O2, for Fe-CDs; (D) Lieweaver-Burk plots of Fe-CDs with TMB or (F) H2O2 as substrate. (Data were presented as mean ± SD n = 3).

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the Fe-CDs + TMB + H2O2 system after adding various concentrations of TA; (B) The linear plots of the change in absorbance (ΔA) versus the concentration of TA; (C) The photoluminescence spectra of the Fe-CDs + TMB + H2O2 system post-addition of varied TA concentrations; (D) The linear plots of (FF0)/F0 as a function of TA concentration. (Data were presented as mean ± SD n = 3).
Detection of TA concentration in actual samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tannic acid content calculated by the Fe-CDs sensing platform.
Tannic acid content calculated by the national standard of NYT 1600–2008.

head.ce:title: Iron-doped carbon dots with peroxidase activity for colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal detection of tannic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, 430072, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing, 100190, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the indispensable role and diverse applications of tannic acid in the food industry, the development of an efficient, precise, and rapid detection and measurement technology for tannic acid is of paramount importance. In this research, we presented the fabrication of iron-incorporated carbon dots (Fe-CDs) through a single-step hydrothermal method, employing Schiff base-structured iron complexes (Fe-Salen) and citric acid (CA) as precursors. Leveraging the catalytic properties of Fe-CDs, we developed a strategy that integrates colorimetric and fluorescence dual signal detection techniques to detect tannic acid (TA). At the heart of this strategy is the peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity exhibited by Fe-CDs, which effectively catalyzes the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into its visually discernible blue oxidized form (ox-TMB), in the existence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Upon the addition of TA, the blue solution undergoes discoloration, enabling colorimetric detection. Furthermore, the interaction between Fe-CDs, TMB, and H2O2 significantly quenches the luminescence of Fe-CDs, and the recovery of fluorescence upon the addition of TA introduces an additional dimension of fluorescence signal to the detection process. This dual-signal detection mode not only enhances the sensitivity and accuracy of the assay but also broadens the potential applications of Fe-CDs in fields such as biological analysis and food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tannic acid (TA), a polyphenolic natural compound derived from variety of plants [1], exhibits diverse biological activities such as antioxidant, antibiotic, and anti-inflammatory [2]. It is widely employed in medicine, health products, cosmetics, food additives, and leather industry [3]. In food processing, tannic acid can prevent oxidation and deterioration, thus extending the shelf life of food. Meanwhile, it can also inhibit the growth and reproduction of some harmful microorganisms, improving the safety of food [4]. In addition, tannic acid enhances the aroma and flavor of foods including red wine, beer, chocolate, and jam during processing. However, excessive intake of tannic acid can also lead to diarrhea, vomiting, and abdominal discomfort [5,6]. Therefore, paying proper attention to the tannic acid containing food is imperative in daily life [7]. As such, the precise detection of tannic acid plays a pivotal role in ensuring food safety and promoting human health. So far, different methods for TA detection including differential pulse voltammetry [8], High-resolution liquid chromatography [9], fluorometry [10], and colorimetry [11] are utilized. Among these methods, fluorescence spectroscopy and colorimetry are particularly favored for their simplicity, high sensitivity, and low equipment requirements [12]. For instance, Ahmed et al [13] developed a fluorescence sensor founded on fluorescent carbon dots for the detection of TA in wine. Xie et al [14] reported a colorimetric sensor founded on Cu single-atom nanozymes for rapid detection of TA. Currently, the application of dual-signal output methods in the field of TA detection is scarcely reported, necessitating the urgent development of a dual-signal detection system for TA.

Carbon dots (CDs) are tiny carbon-based nanoparticles, with a particle diameter of smaller than 10 nm [15]. Due to their low cytotoxicity, easy surface modification, and tunable fluorescence property [16–18], they are extensively used in cell imaging, photoelectric catalysis, analytical detection, and other fields [19]. CDs are often doped with metal ions or non-metal ions to alter their intrinsic properties [20,21]. Since metal ions hold more electrons and unoccupied orbitals than heteroatoms, the doping with metal ions can change the electron density distribution of CDs, thereby allowing the regulation of their physical and chemical properties [22–24]. Presently, the synthesis of metal-doped carbon dots face several challenges. For example, the redox property of metal ions can adversely affect the synthesis process. During the solvothermal process, metal ions tend to form nano-metal oxides, which can interfere with the synthesis of carbon dots, hindering the progress of the reaction and consequently reducing its efficiency and yield [22,25]. To improve the productivity and performance of metal-doped carbon quantum dots, Schiff base metal complexes with excellent coordination capability and stability were chosen as raw materials for the synthesis of doping carbon dots.

Herein, the Fe-CDs with excellent POD-like activity was prepared through a simple hydrothermal approach with iron Schiff base complex (Fe-Salen) and citric acid (CA) as precursors. In the existence of H2O2, Fe-CDs catalyzed the oxidation of TMB into its blue-colored product (ox-TMB). Additionally, the interaction of Fe-CDs with TMB and H2O2 resulted in fluorescence quenching. By leveraging the POD-like activity as well as fluorescence attributes of Fe-CDs, a dual-signal colorimetric-fluorescence method for TA detection was established. The detection limits of the colorimetric and fluorescence approaches were determined to be 0.024 and 0.0082 μM, respectively. In summary, this work showcased the synthesis and sensing applications of iron-doped carbon dots, emphasizing the merits of easy-to-perform synthesis and high detection sensitivity and selectivity.

The synthesis of Fe-CDs was achieved using a hydrothermal method. Initially, 50 mg of Fe-Salen and 500 mg of CA were completely dissolved in 50 mL of DMF. The mixture was subsequently poured into a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and subjected to heating at 160 °C for 8 h, followed by cooling to ambient temperature. The resulting mixture was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to allow the sedimentation of larger particles. The supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm polyether sulfone membrane to eliminate finer particulates. The filtered solution was placed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag and dialyzed with ultrapure water for 36 h. Eventually, the dialysate was freeze-dried to get Fe-CDs powder (Fig. 1 ). For comparison, undoped CDs were also prepared following the same protocol, with CA as the sole precursor.

The peroxidase-mimicking property of Fe-CDs were evaluated by examining their catalytic potential in the oxidation of TMB or o-PD in the existence of H2O2 [26]. Specifically, 100 μL each of H2O2 (10 mM), TMB (5 mM), and Fe-CDs (1 mg/mL) were introduced into 2700 μL of CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.8). After 5 min of reaction, the UV–Vis absorption at 652 nm was recorded, and the changes in the solution's color were observed. Moreover, to elucidate the impact of assorted factors regarding the peroxidase-mimicking activity of Fe-CDs, parameters such as pH, temperature, and reaction duration were varied and the corresponding effects were recorded. Similarly, 100 μL each of H2O2 (10 mM), o-PD (5 mM), and Fe-CDs (1 mg/mL) were added to 2700 μL of CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer (0.2 M, pH 3.8). After 5-min incubation, the UV–Vis absorption changes beyond 420 nm and color variations in o-phenylenediamine were detected.

The kinetic parameters associated with the peroxidase-mimicking activity of Fe-CDs were investigated by employing TMB and H2O2 as substrate molecules. In particular, 100 μL of Fe-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) was introduced into a colorimetric tube, followed by the addition of varying concentrations of H2O2 while maintaining a constant TMB concentration (0.16 mM), or the addition of different concentrations of TMB while keeping the H2O2 concentration constant (0.33 mM). The reaction bulk of the mixed solution was adjusted to 3.0 mL employing CH3COOH/CH3COONa solution (0.2 M, pH 3.8). Afterward, the absorption changes of the solution at 652 nm were recorded every 60 s. The kinetic constants, namely Vmax (maximal reaction velocity) and Km (the Michaelis constant), were established according to the Michaelis−Menten equation [27]:
V = Vmax
S
/
Km +
S
V represents the starting velocity, Vmax represents the maximum reaction velocity, [S] indicates the substrate concentration (H2O2 or TMB), and Km represents the Michaelis-Menten constant.

In a 2.7 mL CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 3.8), 100 μL each of TMB, H2O2, and Fe-CDs were added separately, followed by incubation for 6 min. Subsequently, the spectral absorption of the resulting solution, covering the wavelength from 550 to 750 nm, was recorded. Afterward, various concentrations of TA (0.25–2 μM) were added, and the variations in absorbance at a wavelength of 652 nm were measured and recorded. Additionally, the selectivity of the colorimetric method for TA detection was investigated. Ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), tea polyphenols (TP), anthocyanins (ACNs), chlorogenic acid (CGA), quercetin (QCT), K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, FA, Gly, Arg, Glu, lactose, fructose, and maltose (each at 5 mM) were selected as substitutes for TA, and their measurements were performed using the same method as described above.

In a 2.7 mL CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer solution (0.2 M, pH 3.8), 100 μL each of TMB, H2O2, and Fe-CDs were added and incubated for 6 min. The PL spectrum of the final solution was tested with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Then, different concentrations of TA were added, and the fluorescence recovery factor (FF0)/F0 was recorded, where F and F0 represent the PL intensity of Fe-CDs in the existence and absence of TA, respectively. The selectivity of the fluorometric method for TA detection was also studied. Various potential interferents, including ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), tea polyphenols (TP), anthocyanins (ACNs), chlorogenic acid (CGA), quercetin (QCT), K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, FA, Gly, Arg, Glu, lactose, fructose, and maltose (each at a concentration of 5 mM), were selected, and the measurements were performed following the aforementioned procedure.

At least three parallel groups were set for each experimental group, and data were plotted as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance was determined using t-test, and *p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The iron complex (Fe-Salen) was synthesized through a conventional reaction with high yield (see Supporting Information). The Fe-CDs were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method, with Fe-Salen as both nitrogen and iron sources, and CA as the carbon source. The HR-TEM images (Fig. 2A) demonstrated that the synthesized Fe-CDs exhibit spherical morphology with uniform dispersion [28], with an average diameter of 2.31 nm (Fig. S3a). Additionally, the FT-IR spectroscopy was utilized to characterize the surface functional groups present on Fe-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, the peak at 3426 cm−1 was imputed to C-OH/O-H stretching vibration, while peaks at 2928, 1624, and 1455 cm−1 were attributed to C-H/C=O and CN stretching vibrations [29]. Moreover, the peak signals at 1125 and 652 cm−1 were corresponded to FeN and FeO stretching vibrations [30], respectively. Furthermore, the structure and chemical composition of Fe-CDs were further characterized by XPS, and the results are illustrated in Fig. S3b. The XPS full spectrum confirmed the existence of C, N, O, and Fe in Fe-CDs, with element contents of 70.87 % (C), 6.2 % (N), 22.27 % (O), and 0.65 % (Fe), respectively. The C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 2C revealed six carbon species including CC at 284.11 eV, CC at 284.8 eV, CN at 285.66 eV, CO at 286.54 eV, CN at 287.71 eV, and CO at 288.71 eV [31]. In the N1s spectrum presented in Fig. 2D, peaks were observed at 399.17, 399.94, and 400.74 eV, corresponding to CN, CN, and NH, respectively. Similarly, the O1s (Fig. 2E) spectrum exhibited three distinct peaks at 531.19, 532.11, and 533.37 eV, ascribed to CO, C-OH and OH groups respectively [32]. Furthermore, the Fe 2p (Fig. 2F) spectrum was matched to four peaks at 709.32, 722.47, 711.16, and 724.43 eV, corresponding to Fe2+ 2p3/2, Fe2+ 2p1/2, Fe3+ 2p3/2, and Fe3+ 2p1/2 [33], respectively. Collectively, these findings validated the successful doping of Fe and the presence of Fe-, N-, and O-containing surface functionalities in Fe-CDs. Besides, the zeta potential of Fe-CDs was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S3c, the zeta potential of Fe-CDs was measured to be −25.96 mV, indicating that the surface is negatively charged, which may be due to the ionisation of oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g. -COOH, -OH) on the surface of the material.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were utilized to investigate the optical property of Fe-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 3A, the absorption peak of Fe-CDs at 270 nm can be ascribed to the transition induced by CC in the sp2 domain, which emits cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV illumination in DMF solution. The PL emission spectrum exhibits independence from excitation wavelengths from 330 to 370 nm (Fig. 3B), the maximum excitation wavelength is 360 nm, with an emission peak at 520 nm (Fig. 3C). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Fe-CDs using quinine sulfate as the reference [34], was determined to be 11.5 % in DMF (Fig. 3D-3E). The fluorescence stability of Fe-CDs under varying NaCl concentrations, temperatures, and storage durations was tested. The PL intensity remained unaltered across NaCl concentrations ranging from 0 to 1.5 M and temperatures from 10 to 60 °C (Fig. S4a-b). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity remained stable after 10 days of storage at room temperature (Fig. S4c). These results demonstrated that Fe-CDs possess outstanding fluorescence stability.

POD enzymes are widely recognized for their potent catalytic abilities, enabling them to facilitate the oxidation reaction of TMB or o-PD in the presence of H2O2, resulting in the formation of visibly colored blue or yellow products [22]. Therefore, the peroxidase-mimicking activity of Fe-CDs was investigated employing TMB and o-PD as chromogenic substrates. As shown in Fig. 4A, a blue coloration accompanied by a distinct characteristic absorption peak emerged at 652 nm when Fe-CDs, TMB, and H₂O₂ were all present in the solution. Conversely, no color change occurred when Fe-CDs were blended with either one of the substrates alone or when the two substrates were combined without Fe-CDs. Similarly, when Fe-CDs + o-PD + H2O2 were combined, a yellow color appeared with a noticeable absorption peak at 420 nm (Fig. 4B). This revealed that Fe-CDs possessed the ability to catalyze the production of oxidative species from H2O2, facilitating the conversion of TMB into ox-TMB and o-PD into its oxidized form. Furthermore, the iron-undoped carbon dots (CDs) was prepared as control. Compared to Fe-CDs, CDs were unable to catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2, suggesting that iron ions have a crucial impact on the catalytic process (Fig. S5a). The POD activity of Fe-CDs was then assessed by different reaction time (5–50 min), pH value (2.6–7), and temperature (10–45 °C). As indicated in Fig. S5b-d, the catalytic activity of Fe-CDs attained its optimum level at 30 min of reaction, with pH value of 3.8 and temperature of 30 °C. To study the stability of Fe-CDs, the interference of different coexisting substances was explored. As shown in Fig. S6a, the catalytic system was barely affected by other coexisting substances. Moreover, Fe-CDs kept significant POD-like activity even after 15 days of storage (Fig. S6b), indicating their excellent stability, favoring their application in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of kinetics and catalytic mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a comprehensive understanding of the catalytic properties of Fe-CDs, a kinetic analysis at steady state was conducted under the best reaction conditions. By varying the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB, the Michaelis-Menten models were established (Fig. 4C-F). The double reciprocal plot method was employed to determine the Vmax and Km values of both substrates. Notably, the Km value of TMB was calculated to be 0.168 mM, which was slightly lower than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (0.434 mM), while the Km for H2O2 (0.105 mM) was nearly 35 times lower than that of HRP (3.70 mM) [35]. Furthermore, a comparison of Fe-CDs with previously reported mimic enzymes (Table S1) revealed that Fe-CDs displayed superior peroxidase-mimicking activity.

To explore the catalytic mechanism of Fe-CDs, various scavengers were employed, including D-histidine, p-benzoquinone, and isopropanol, which target 1O2, •O2 −, and •OH, respectively. The notable alteration in absorbance at 652 nm within the reaction system was exclusively observed upon the incorporation of p-benzoquinone (Fig. S7a), indicating that Fe-CDs catalyze the oxidation of H2O2 to generate •O2 −. In addition, electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments further validate the generated ROS species. As shown in Fig. S7b, the ESR spectra of the DMPO + H2O2 + Fe-CDs system showed the typical signals of DMPO/•O2 −, indicating the generation of •O2 −. These results demonstrated that Fe-CDs could act as peroxidase-like enzyme to catalyze the production of •O2 −by H2O2 in the reaction system.

The accurate and rapid determination of TA content is critical for ensuring food safety. Based on the antioxidant effect of tannic acid, the blue ox-TMB can be reduced to colorless TMB. According to this phenomenon, a colorimetric approach was established for the detection of tannic acid. As illustrated in Fig. 5A, with the addition of TA, the absorption peak intensity at 652 nm gradually diminishes, and the blue color of the solution gradually fades to colorless. The absorbance change exhibits a strong linear correlation with TA concentration. When the concentration ranges from 0.25 to 1 μM, the linear relationship between absorbance and TA concentration is described by the equation ΔA = 0.97 [TA] + 0.0855 (R2 = 0.998). Furthermore, for TA concentrations spanning from 1 to 2 μM, the linear relationship is expressed as ΔA = 0.232 [TA] + 0.8294 (R2 = 0.999) (Fig. 5B). The detection limit achieved by this method is 0.024 μM, which is comparable to the colorimetric methods described in the literature (Table S2). The selectivity of Fe-CDs for TA identifying was rigorously evaluated employing a wide array of potential interfering substances, including AA, GSH, TP, ACNs, CGA, QCT, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, FA, Gly, Arg, Glu, lactose, fructose, and maltose. Remarkably, even at concentrations 10 times higher than that of TA, these interfering substances caused no significant change in absorbance at 652 nm (Fig. S8a and S8d).

Beyond the color change observed in the solution, a significant fluorescence quenching occurs when TMB + H2O2 + Fe-CDs are present simultaneously. In contrast, no fluorescence quenching is discernible in TMB + Fe-CDs or Fe-CDs + H2O2 systems. Moreover, the fluorescence recovers after adding TA to the TMB + H2O2 + Fe-CDs system (Fig. S8b). Based on these phenomena, a fluorescent analysis method for detecting TA through the fluorescence on-off-on transition of Fe-CDs is established. As depicted in Fig. 5C, with the addition of varying concentrations of tannic acid, the fluorescence of Fe-CDs progressively recovers. When the concentration of TA ranges from 0.25 to 2 μM, a linear equation of (F – F0)/F0 = 9.67883 [TA] – 1.37932 (R2 = 0.992) was obtained (Fig. 5D). Based on the aforementioned linear equation, the limit of detection for TA is established at 0.0082 μM, which is comparable to the fluorescence methods reported in literature (Table S2). The selectivity of Fe-CDs in detecting TA was tested against potential interfering substances, e.g., AA, GSH, TP, ACNs, CGA, QCT, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, FA, Gly, Arg, Glu, lactose, fructose, and maltose potential interfering substances. As shown in Fig. S8c and S8e, the fluorescence recovery is not significant even when the concentration of interfering substances is 10 times the concentration of TA. These results indicated that the selected interferences have little impact on the determination of TA, demonstrating the satisfactory anti-interference ability of the proposed fluorescence method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TA in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Green tea and wine were chosen as real-world samples to validate the capability of Fe-CDs in detecting TA. Before measurement, the samples were filtered and diluted to appropriate concentrations. The results, presented in Table 1 , indicate that both the colorimetric and fluorescent approach achieved high recovery rates ranging from 98.12 % to 104.47 %, with all RSD values <6.0 %. These findings demonstrated that the colorimetric and fluorescent methods based on Fe-CDs possesses high precision and reliability in detecting TA, making it suitable for the detection of TA in actual samples. To ensure the accuracy and reliability of the analytical results, the results obtained from the Fe-CDs sensing system were compared with those obtained by the standard method. A shown in Table 1, the results from Fe-CQDs system were consistent with the national industry standard method based on spectrophotometry, indicating that the proposed method has good accuracy.

In summary, this study reports the preparation of Fe-CDs by a single-step hydrothermal way, using Schiff base-structured iron complexes and CA as raw materials. Due to the effective incorporation of iron the resulting Fe-CDs showed remarkable peroxidase-like activity, enabling them to catalyze the oxidation of TMB into blue ox-TMB in the existence of H2O2. Leveraging the dual changes in absorption and fluorescence signals of Fe-CDs, the highly selective and sensitive detection methods for tannic acid (TA) were established, with detection limits (LOD) of 0.024 μM (colorimetry) and 0.0082 μM (fluoremetry), respectively. Additionally, this strategy was successfully employed for TA determination in green tea and wine samples with high accuracy. Thus, this work not only developed a new strategy to prepare metal-doped carbon dots using Schiff base-structured iron complexes as precursor but also expanded their application in detection leveraging their remarkable POD-like activity.

Xiang Deng: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaowei Qu: Supervision, Methodology. Muneeb Ur Rehman: Writing – review & editing. Zhiyuan Hu: Visualization, Supervision. Fengshou Wu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors reported no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (grant no. 21601142), Hubei Province Science and Technology Innovation Talent Plan Project (grant no. 2024DJC036). X. Deng acknowledges the financial support from the 15th Graduate Innovative Fund of Wuhan Institute of Technology (grant no. CX2023005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2025.112481.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Applications of tannic acid in membrane technologies: a review
Adv. Colloid Interf. Sci.

Trends Food Sci. Technol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances in tannic acid-incorporated biomaterials: infection treatment, regenerative medicine, cancer therapy, and biosensing
Chem. Eng. J.

N.; Sharifi, S.; Rabiee, N.; Ersoy, S.; Maleki, A.; Zare, E.
